incorrects.description: Student selected 'Different items inside': misread what changed between the two groups

params.description: Left visual highlights: bags pulse to draw attention.
Right visual highlights: bags pulse to draw attention.

steps.text: Look at the bags themselves. The items inside are the same. What about the number of bags?

condition.selected: One has more total items inside

incorrects.description: Student selected 'One has more total items inside': focused on total rather than structure

steps.text: The total is the same. But look at how the groups are organized. Count the bags on each side.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Nothing—they're the same': did not notice structural difference

steps.text: They look similar, but something is different. Count how many bags are on the left. Now count the bags on the right.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Left visual: bags count from 1 to 3.
Right visual: bags count from 1 to 5.

steps.text: Let me show you. On the left, I count the bags: 1, 2, 3. Three bags. On the right, I count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five bags. The number of bags is different.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 × 4: reversed the order of groups and items per group

params.description: The 4 boxes highlight to emphasize the number of groups.

steps.text: You've got the right numbers. But in multiplication, order matters for what we're describing. Count the boxes first. How many groups are there?

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 + 2: used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: All items in all boxes highlight to show the total count.

steps.text: Addition puts amounts together. But here we have equal groups. When you see equal groups, multiplication is the tool. How many groups, and how many in each?

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 + 4: used addition and reversed the order

params.description: The 4 boxes highlight to show the structure of equal groups.

steps.text: These aren't two separate amounts to add. They're equal groups. Multiplication describes equal groups. Count the boxes, then count what's in one box.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Animation counts the 4 boxes, then counts 2 items in one box, building 4 × 2.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, count the boxes: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four groups. Then look inside one box: 2 items. The expression is 4 times 2. That's how multiplication captures equal groups.
